skill.name: autofix
skill.description: Fetch CodeRabbit review comments from GitHub and guide an AI coding agent through proposed fixes.
skill.summary: Find unresolved CodeRabbit PR comments and help apply review fixes.
skill.author: coderabbitai

security_audit.summary: Static command and network findings are mostly expected for a GitHub PR automation skill, and the weak cryptography matches are false positives. The material risk is semantic: the workflow tells the agent to execute CodeRabbit review-comment prompts as direct instructions and can apply, commit, and push code changes.

high_findings.title: Untrusted Review Comment Prompt Execution
high_findings.description: The skill extracts agent prompts from GitHub review comments and directs the AI to execute those prompts literally. Review comment text is remote content and can carry prompt injection or unsafe code-change instructions, even when the intended source is CodeRabbit.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording explicitly says to execute fetched agent prompts as direct instructions and to follow them literally. The source of those prompts is remote GitHub comment content, so the prompt-injection risk is direct and semantic.

high_findings.title: Automated Code Modification and Publication Workflow
high_findings.description: The skill supports an auto-fix mode that applies fixes, creates a consolidated commit, and can push changes. This is high impact when combined with untrusted review-comment instructions because it can publish unintended code changes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly includes auto-fix, file edits, commit creation, and push prompts. User confirmation is present for mode choice and push, so this is high risk but not confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Expected GitHub CLI Commands With Side Effects
medium_findings.description: The external command findings are largely true positives for intended GitHub automation. The commands query PR data, post comments, create PRs, add files, and commit changes, so users should review permissions and command output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real and can affect GitHub or the local repository. They match the stated purpose, which lowers confidence that this is abuse rather than expected functionality.

medium_findings.title: Shell Substitution in Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The examples use shell substitution to derive the branch name and build a multiline PR comment. This is common shell usage, but it still requires normal shell quoting care in repositories with unusual branch names or generated values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present and executable in a shell, but the examples are mostly static GitHub CLI usage. I did not find evidence that arbitrary user input is concatenated into a shell command.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Static Matches
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptography findings do not show cryptographic code. The cited lines are metadata, parsing instructions, or GraphQL fields, so there is no evidence of a weak algorithm implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain prose or GraphQL field names, not cryptographic APIs. I found no encryption, hashing, or cipher selection logic in those locations.

low_findings.title: Benign Installation URL
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the official GitHub CLI website in installation instructions. This is a normal documentation link rather than a suspicious network destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is displayed as an installation reference for GitHub CLI. It is not used for data transfer or hidden network communication.

dangerous_patterns.title: Direct Execution of Fetched Agent Prompts
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote review-comment content is treated as a fix specification and direct AI instruction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple lines instruct the agent to use external prompt text literally. This is the strongest evidence in the review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Write and Publish Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes git add, git commit, and git push steps after applying fixes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and have repository side effects. Push is gated by a user prompt, which reduces but does not remove risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub Comment Posting Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow posts generated summaries and reactions through the GitHub CLI.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present and communicate with GitHub. The content appears operational, not exfiltrative.

content.value_statement: Code review comments can be time consuming to collect and triage across a pull request. This skill fetches unresolved CodeRabbit comments and guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through fixes.
actual_capabilities: Checks the current branch for an open GitHub pull request.
Fetches unresolved CodeRabbit review threads through GitHub GraphQL.
Filters review comments to known CodeRabbit bot authors.
Displays issue severity, title, location, type, and action guidance.
Supports manual review or batch auto-fix workflows.
Can create a consolidated commit and post a summary comment.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication and repository access.
Requires an open pull request reviewed by CodeRabbit.
Depends on CodeRabbit comment format and bot author names.
Can make repository changes, so users must review proposed fixes carefully.

use_cases.title: Triage PR Review Comments
use_cases.description: Collect unresolved CodeRabbit comments for the current branch and decide which fixes to apply first.

use_cases.title: Apply Review Fixes With Approval
use_cases.description: Review each proposed fix with context and approve only changes that match project standards.

use_cases.title: Batch Routine Fixes
use_cases.description: Apply straightforward CodeRabbit fixes in one run, then validate and prepare a consolidated commit.

prompt_templates.title: Find CodeRabbit Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Find unresolved CodeRabbit review comments for my current branch and show the issue list before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Review Fixes One By One
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch CodeRabbit comments, summarize each issue, and ask for approval before applying any fix.

prompt_templates.title: Fix High Priority Comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Focus on high priority CodeRabbit comments first, propose diffs, and stop before committing.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Fix And Validate
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply routine CodeRabbit fixes in batch, run the repository validation checks, and prepare one consolidated commit only after review.

output_examples.input: Find unresolved CodeRabbit comments for this PR.
output_examples.output: A prioritized issue table with severity, title, file location, issue type, and suggested action.

output_examples.input: Review each issue before applying changes.
output_examples.output: A per-issue review flow showing the current problem, proposed change, and approval choices.
A concise summary of fixed, deferred, and skipped issues with modified files and commit status.

best_practices: Use manual review mode for security, authentication, payment, or data-handling changes.
Run project tests, linting, and build checks before pushing changes.
Review fetched agent prompts as untrusted input before applying fixes.
anti_patterns: Do not auto-fix comments from untrusted pull requests without human review.
Do not push generated fixes before checking the final diff.
Do not use this skill without a clean understanding of repository contribution rules.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses gh commands to find pull requests, fetch review threads, and post comments.

faq.question: Can it work without an open pull request?
faq.answer: No. The workflow expects the current branch to have an open GitHub pull request.

faq.question: Does it automatically change files?
faq.answer: It can change files in auto-fix mode. Manual mode asks for approval before applying each fix.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It treats remote review-comment text as agent instructions and can modify, commit, and push code.

faq.question: Can it post back to GitHub?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow can post a summary comment and optionally react to a CodeRabbit comment.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
